content.text: The debt impact prioritization framework converts the subjective judgment of "how important is this debt item?" into a reproducible, score-based decision. Without a framework, debt prioritisation is driven by whoever argues most forcefully in sprint planning, which tends to favour debt that is visible and frustrating over debt that is high-impact but less visible. A score-based framework ensures that a P1 assignment requires meeting objective criteria, not just someone's strong opinion.

attrs.alt: Debt prioritization score = Impact × Urgency × Breadth, with P1 ≥30, P2 15-29, P3 5-14, P4 1-4

content.text: The three-factor model scores debt on Impact (severity of the problem when it manifests), Urgency (how quickly the debt is compounding), and Breadth (how many users or use cases are affected). Multiplying the three produces a score from 1 (low-impact, stable, narrow) to 45 (user-facing failure, compounding daily, all users). The score drives the priority tier assignment, which drives the sprint allocation timeline.

Each scoring dimension requires a precise definition to be applied consistently across the team. Without definitions, the same item will be scored differently by different engineers, producing inconsistent priority assignments. The definitions below are calibrated for Claude integration teams; they can be adjusted to fit a specific organisation's context, but the adjustments should be documented and applied consistently.

The COMPOUNDING_DAILY urgency score applies to prompt debt that accumulates a patch with every sprint cycle and to monitoring gaps that allow problems to grow undetected. The LATENT_RISK impact score is the most commonly underestimated: a missing circuit breaker scores LATENT_RISK because it is not causing failures now, but under specific conditions (upstream API degradation) it will cause a cascading failure. Teams that default to scoring latent risks as FRICTION understate their priority and allow them to accumulate until the foreseeable condition materialises.

Abstract scoring criteria are only useful if the team can apply them consistently to real debt items. The examples below cover the five Claude-specific debt categories and show how the scoring produces priority assignments that match the intuitive importance of each item.
System prompt generates hallucinations on date-format input
No retry logic on API rate limit errors
Model version unpinned; upgrade may break behaviour
3 (daily — risk grows with each day unpinned)
Golden set has 5 evals; should have 50
No p99 latency alert; slow responses go undetected

The model version unpinned item scores P1 because the urgency is COMPOUNDING_DAILY: every day the model is unpinned is a day when Anthropic could release a model update that changes behaviour and the team would not know until user complaints arrive. This is counterintuitive because the debt item is not currently causing a problem — it is a latent risk. The urgency multiplier converts a latent risk with a compounding exposure into a P1 priority, which correctly reflects that the cost of not addressing it grows daily.

The score-based framework produces a default priority tier. In some cases the team may have contextual information that justifies overriding the scored tier. A P3 item that is in a code path that is about to receive major new development should be bumped to P2 because addressing it now is cheaper than addressing it after the new development has layered additional code on top. A P1 item that requires a platform team dependency that will not be available for six weeks may be acknowledged at P1 but held for the dependency, with the blocked status recorded in the register.

The override_valid check enforces that overrides are not silent. An override without a reason and an approver is indistinguishable from a scoring error — it is a number that does not match the formula without explanation. By requiring override_reason and override_approved_by before accepting an override as valid, the framework ensures that deviations from the computed score are documented decisions rather than ad hoc adjustments. The full_record_line method makes the override visible in any register report, so reviewers can see which items have been overridden and evaluate whether the override justification still applies.

A debt item's priority score is not permanent. The three dimensions can change as the context evolves: a LATENT_RISK item becomes USER_FACING_FAIL if a related incident occurs; a STABLE urgency becomes COMPOUNDING_QUARTERLY after a model update changes the behaviour the debt was designed to work around; a SUBSET breadth becomes ALL_USERS after a feature flag enables the affected code path for all users. The register should be reviewed quarterly for items whose scores are stale, and the quarterly debt review is the structured moment for this recalibration.

The escalations_this_quarter method identifies debt items that were upgraded to a higher priority tier during the current quarter. A quarter with many escalations is a signal that the team is not catching debt early enough: items that were P3 or P4 are becoming P1 or P2 because the context changed before they were addressed. The patterns method distils the reasons behind escalations, which often reveals a systemic trigger — a model update that invalidated several assumptions, a scale increase that turned latent risks into actual failures, or a new feature that extended an affected code path to more users.
Applying the Framework to Prompt Refactoring Decisions

Prompt refactoring is a special case of debt resolution that requires the prioritization framework to answer two distinct questions: whether to refactor now, and which parts of the prompt to address first. A system prompt with 12 patches and a high overall COMPOUNDING_DAILY urgency score may contain individual instructions with very different impact and breadth profiles. The framework applies at the instruction level as well as the prompt level, enabling the team to triage within a refactor sprint rather than treating the entire prompt as a single undifferentiated task.

The refactor_order method provides a sequenced plan for a prompt refactor sprint. Starting with P1 instructions ensures the highest-impact improvements are made first; if the sprint runs short, the P3 and P4 instructions can be deferred without compromising the critical improvements. The removals list identifies instructions that are obsolete and can be deleted without replacement — often the most valuable output of a prompt refactor, because removing dead instructions reduces the cognitive complexity of the remaining prompt without requiring careful rewrites. A well-structured refactor sprint that works through this list in priority order produces a significantly cleaner prompt in a finite, bounded time.
Team Calibration to Reduce Scoring Variance

A prioritization framework is only as consistent as the team applying it. Individual engineers bring different reference points to impact and urgency scoring: one engineer's LATENT_RISK is another's QUALITY_DEGRADED, and the gap produces inconsistent priority assignments for similar items. Calibration sessions — short exercises where the team scores the same set of hypothetical items independently and then compares — surface these gaps and produce shared definitions. Running a calibration session at the start of each quarter, before the quarterly debt review, ensures that the team's shared understanding of each dimension is current.

Choose one item from each debt category — prompt, integration, test, model management, ops — that the team has real familiarity with. Hypothetical items produce less useful calibration than items the team has actually debated.
Each team member scores all five items on all three dimensions without discussing with others. Use a shared form that collects scores before showing the aggregate.

For any item with more than one tier of variance in any dimension (e.g., two engineers score Impact as 3 and another as 5), the team discusses what each person considered and agrees on the correct score and why.

If the discussion reveals that the written definitions did not capture an important distinction, update them. The calibration session is the primary mechanism for keeping the framework aligned with the team's evolving context.

The calibration session takes 30 minutes quarterly and prevents the drift that makes priority scores unreliable over time. Without calibration, the framework's categories gradually expand or contract based on whichever engineer is doing the scoring that week, and the P1/P2 boundary loses its meaning. With calibration, the team maintains a shared reference point that makes the framework a reliable tool for sprint planning rather than a formality.

The debt impact prioritization framework converts subjective debt triage into a reproducible three-factor score: Impact (1–5) × Urgency (1–3) × Breadth (1–3). Scores of 30+ map to P1 (current sprint), 15–29 to P2 (next 1–2 sprints), 5–14 to P3 (within quarter), and 1–4 to P4 (best-effort). The ImpactScore, UrgencyScore, and BreadthScore enums provide precise definitions that eliminate inter-engineer scoring inconsistency. Scored examples for six Claude-specific debt types — system prompt hallucination, missing retry logic, unpinned model version, inadequate golden set, hardcoded API parameters, and missing latency alerting — illustrate how the formula produces priority assignments aligned with intuitive urgency. The ScoredDebtRecord structure extends the base record with computed tier and optional override, requiring documented reason and approver for any departure from the formula.
